data.title: Astronova outlines fiscal 2026 growth targets driven by aerospace and PI segment advancements
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: AstroNova, Inc. (ALOT) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Greg Woods, CEO, highlighted challenges faced during fiscal 2025, including the integration of the MTEX acquisition, the Boeing strike, and delayed defense orders. He noted a restructuring plan aimed at achieving $3 million in annual cost savings and emphasized a strategic focus on higher-margin products.
  • AstroNova introduced a name change for its Test & Measurement segment to Aerospace, reflecting its market focus. This segment achieved record revenue of $48.9 million for fiscal 2025, contributing one-third of consolidated sales.
  • The integration of MTEX technology is central to AstroNova's strategy, with plans to launch five next-generation products in fiscal 2026. These include enhanced versions of MTEX label printers and industrial packaging printers under new brands, which are expected to boost consumables-driven revenue.
  • CFO Tom DeByle reported Q4 revenue of $37.4 million, down 5.6% year-over-year, with a gross margin of 34.1%. Operating expenses rose due to a $13.4 million goodwill impairment charge tied to MTEX. Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter was $2.8 million.

Outlook

  • Management projects fiscal 2026 net revenue to range between $160 million and $165 million, representing a 7.4% growth at the midpoint compared to fiscal 2025.
  • Adjusted EBITDA margin is expected between 8.5% and 9.5%, reflecting a 60 basis point improvement at the midpoint.
  • Key growth drivers include the integration of new print engine and ink technology in the PI segment and the transition of OEMs to the ToughWriter printer line in the Aerospace segment.

Financial Results

  • Q4 revenue was $37.4 million, a decline from the prior year due to lower sales in both segments. Gross profit was $12.7 million, with a gross margin of 34.1% compared to 37.2% in Q4 fiscal 2024.
  • The PI segment reported revenue of $25.7 million, with a non-GAAP operating profit of $2.3 million, or 8.9% of revenue. The MTEX acquisition impacted margins negatively.
  • The Aerospace segment's revenue was $11.7 million, impacted by delayed defense orders and the Boeing strike. Segment operating profit was $2.3 million.
  • Liquidity at the end of Q4 stood at $9.7 million, with cash and cash equivalents at $5.1 million. Funded debt decreased to $46.7 million.

Q&A

  • Brandon Daniel, Atai Capital, questioned the inkjet order delay. CEO Woods clarified that the delay was related to the legacy product line and not MTEX technology. He emphasized customer-requested enhancements as the cause.
  • Robert Van Voorhis, Vanatoc Capital Management, asked about the impact of delayed Boeing orders on Aerospace margins. CFO DeByle confirmed their high-margin nature and noted the resumption of these orders.
  • Further clarification was sought on MTEX expenses and their contribution to corporate G&A. CFO DeByle provided detailed figures, including MTEX-specific costs in various categories.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed concerns about integration challenges and margin impacts but showed interest in the strategic initiatives for growth.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, emphasizing decisive actions and future opportunities. CEO Woods highlighted the strategic upside of MTEX and the company's commitment to operational excellence.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone reflected a proactive approach to addressing challenges, while analysts remained cautious.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Fiscal 2025 Q4 guidance was reinstated, with specific targets for fiscal 2026, contrasting with the lack of guidance in Q3. The focus shifted to product launches and integration progress.
  • Analysts maintained a skeptical tone regarding the MTEX integration, consistent with concerns raised in Q3.
  • Revenue decline in Q4 contrasts with the growth reported in Q3, driven by Aerospace performance in the earlier quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Challenges include the prolonged integration of MTEX, delayed defense orders, and the Boeing strike's impact on Aerospace revenue.
  • Management is addressing these risks through restructuring, cost reduction, and product portfolio realignment.
  • Analysts raised concerns about MTEX's financial contribution and the timeline for achieving profitability.

Final Takeaway

AstroNova is navigating integration challenges while advancing its Aerospace and PI segments. With a focus on next-generation product launches and cost-saving measures, the company aims to achieve fiscal 2026 revenue growth and improved margins. Management's proactive stance and strategic initiatives highlight their commitment to long-term shareholder value.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: J&J Q1 2025 Earnings Preview
data.description:
Johnson & Johnson (NYSE: NYSE:JNJ) is set to report its first-quarter earnings on Tuesday, April 15th, before the market opens, with consensus expectations for EPS of $2.58 (-4.8% Y/Y) on revenue of $21.57B (+0.8% Y/Y).

Guggenheim lowered its Q1 estimates ahead of the call, now projecting revenue of $21.63B (from $21.83B) and EPS of $2.59 (from $2.62), citing a complex set of variables including the recent ITCI acquisition, foreign exchange headwinds, and the Medicare Part D redesign. These factors are expected to have mixed short- and long-term effects on JNJ’s earnings outlook.

For full-year 2025, Guggenheim raised its revenue forecast to $90.7B (from $89.7B) but trimmed its EPS estimate to $10.47 (from $10.54), driven largely by the inclusion of ITCI into its model starting. J&J’s current FY25 guidance calls for sales between $89.9B–$90.7B and EPS of $10.25–$10.45, including around $700M in ITCI sales and $0.25 EPS dilution from ITCI.

The brokerage maintained its Neutral rating on the stock, citing concerns around talc litigation and loss of exclusivity for major brands like Stelara, Xarelto, Opsumit, and Uptravi. However, the firm notes positive momentum in JNJ’s Innovative Medicine pipeline, particularly in immunology and oncology, which could offer long-term upside.

According to BofA Securities, key areas of focus during the earnings call will likely include: updated 2025 guidance, factoring in the ITCI dilution and   FX adjustments. Tariffs are a major concern across the sector, the brokerage said, adding that JNJ could feel the impact sooner due to its MedTech exposure.

Stelara biosimilars, along with progress on the broader pipeline and recent product launches, could be other areas of focus, BofA Securities added, reiterating its Neutral rating on the stock.

While JNJ has a history of strong execution—beating EPS and revenue estimates in every quarter over the past two years—analyst sentiment has cooled. In the last three months, there have been four EPS estimate cuts and six revenue downgrades, pointing to caution heading into the print.

Year-to-date, JNJ shares have advanced nearly 6%.

data.title: Crypto exchange Kraken takes on Robinhood with stock trading launch in U.S.
data.description:
Crypto exchange Kraken is stepping into the traditional finance world, rolling out commission-free trading over more than 11K U.S. stocks and exchange-traded funds, it said on Monday, in a direct challenge to brokerage platforms like Robinhood Markets (HOOD).

Preparing to go public next year, the new ability enables traders to rotate between digital assets and stocks on Kraken's platform.

In a partnership with Alpaca, Kraken launched traditional securities trading in certain states, including New Jersey, Connecticut, Wyoming, Oklahoma, Idaho, Iowa, Rhode Island, Kentucky, Alabama and the District of Columbia.

This initial phase is expected to be followed by a wider rollout to all eligible U.S. clients, according to a statement, with plans to eventually expand to the U.K., Europe and Australia.

“Expanding into equities is a natural step for us, and paves the way for the tokenization of assets," said Kraken Co-CEO Arjun Sethi. "The future of trading is borderless, always on, and built on crypto rails—and Kraken will continue to lead this shift.”

The expansion comes as Robinhood (HOOD) has been beefing up its crypto business, leveraging a crypto-friendly Trump administration to expand into areas like tokenization and other opportunities beyond traditional brokerage. Kraken's move also competes with traditional exchanges like Nasdaq (NDAQ)

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Ulta outlook clouded by trade and spending risks, but not everyone's worried
data.description:
With an uncertain economic backdrop that will undermine discretionary spending coupled with the company’s efforts to expand internationally that are now vulnerable to U.S. trade policy, Ulta Beauty (NASDAQ:ULTA) was downgraded to Hold from Buy at Argus.

While Ulta (NASDAQ:ULTA) is given high marks at Argus based on its debt levels, fixed-cost coverage, profitability, and cash conversion, the company is sensitive to economic factors such as employment and wage growth that impact discretionary spending. It also faces competition from beauty retailers like Sephora, Blue Mercury, and e.l.f. Cosmetics (ELF), as well from mass-market retailers like Target (TGT) and department stores. With this backdrop, Argus analyst Taylor Conrad believes Ulta’s (NASDAQ:ULTA) earnings outlook has turned negative with a 1-year EPS growth forecast of negative 9.63%.

While Conrad is more cautious towards Ulta’s (ULTA) macroeconomic exposure, Wall Street analysts and Seeking Alpha authors are less concerned about Ulta’s growth potential. Seeking Alpha analyst Gary Alexander considers Ulta (ULTA) a “steady ship for unsteady times,” a company that is making slow but steady progress.

“The beauty retailer has been under pressure to repeat its pandemic-era growth, but through careful product assortment, operational overhauls, and a focus on new categories, the company is delivering growth,” Alexander says.

A sentiment echoed by Seeking Alpha analyst Evin Rohrbaugh, adding that despite current political uncertainty and potential recession risks, “Ulta’s diverse product range and strong loyalty program offer resilience.”

While the stock has been idling in a lower range over the past 30-days, it has outperformed the broader market with a gain of 8% in the past month versus a loss of 2.75% for the S&P 500 and -15% for e.l.f. Beauty (ELF).

Year-to-date, however, Ulta’s (ULTA) performance has lagged the S&P as the company has struggled in the past with soft comparable store sales due to stale product assortment and slower growth in the beauty segment.

But with the help of new offerings, a strong fragrance selection, and less promotional pricing, Rohrbaugh views ULTA as a “perfect example of a company elevating itself from good to great,” Rohrbaugh says.

data.title: Bayer is said to consider ending production of Roundup amid legal costs
data.description:
The future of Roundup, the world’s most widely used herbicide, hangs in the balance as chemical maker Bayer (OTCPK:BAYZF) (OTCPK:BAYRY) signals it may soon halt production unless it receives legal protection from ongoing cancer-related lawsuits.

Roundup, based on the active ingredient glyphosate, has long been a staple for farmers and a moneymaker for Bayer (OTCPK:BAYZF) (OTCPK:BAYRY) and its subsidiary Monsanto, which developed the product in the 1970s. But after acquiring Monsanto in 2018, Bayer (OTCPK:BAYZF) (OTCPK:BAYRY) also inherited a deluge of lawsuits alleging that glyphosate causes cancer, a charge the company vigorously denies.

The company is approaching a point where continuing to produce it may not make sense any longer, Bayer (OTCPK:BAYZF) (OTCPK:BAYRY) Chief Executive Bill Anderson said in an interview The Wall Street Journal published on Monday. He suggested that a decision on Roundup could be made within months, not years.

Glyphosate is sprayed on hundreds of millions of acres each year in the U.S. alone, where most corn, soybean and cotton crops are genetically engineered to resist it.

Yet alongside its widespread agricultural use, glyphosate has been a legal and financial headache for Bayer (OTCPK:BAYZF) (OTCPK:BAYRY). Since the acquisition of Monsanto, the company has paid around $10 billion in settlements and still faces about 67,000 lawsuits.

Bayer (OTCPK:BAYZF) (OTCPK:BAYRY) maintains that glyphosate is safe, citing the Environmental Protection Agency and international regulators, but litigation continues to eat into the company’s resources.

Litigation-related losses now reach $2 billion to $3 billion a year, sometimes exceeding Bayer’s (OTCPK:BAYZF) (OTCPK:BAYRY) agricultural R&D spending. While the company brought in $2.8 billion in glyphosate revenue last year, the net return is close to breakeven once legal costs are included, Anderson told the Journal.

Farmers could face shortages

If Bayer (OTCPK:BAYZF) (OTCPK:BAYRY) pulls back, American farmers would be forced to rely on imports from China or switch to other herbicides — some potentially more harmful or less effective. Analyst Joel Jackson of BMO Capital Markets said discontinuing Roundup could also diminish the value of Bayer’s (OTCPK:BAYZF) (OTCPK:BAYRY) proprietary seed technologies, which are designed for glyphosate use. Compounding the issue, Bayer has struggled to win approval for dicamba, a newer herbicide intended to combat glyphosate-resistant weeds.

Pushing for legal shield

To reduce legal liability, Bayer (OTCPK:BAYZF) (OTCPK:BAYRY) has lobbied lawmakers in multiple states to pass legislation protecting pesticide manufacturers from lawsuits if their product labels are approved by the EPA. In March, Georgia became the first state to pass such a bill — pending the governor’s signature.

Just a week later, a Georgia jury ordered Bayer (OTCPK:BAYZF) (OTCPK:BAYRY) to pay $2.1 billion in damages. The company plans to appeal and noted that similar rulings have typically been reduced significantly on appeal.

Critics including attorneys representing tens of thousands of plaintiffs have vowed to challenge the new legal shields in court.

Supreme Court and future uncertainty

Separately, Bayer (OTCPK:BAYZF) (OTCPK:BAYRY) again asked the U.S. Supreme Court to review the legal framework that has allowed state-level lawsuits to proceed despite the EPA's assessment that glyphosate doesn’t warrant a cancer warning. A previous attempt to bring the issue before the Court in 2022 was rejected.

Anderson warned that if Bayer (OTCPK:BAYZF) (OTCPK:BAYRY) discontinues glyphosate, no U.S.-based company is likely to step in due to the legal risks. He also expressed concern that this legal landscape could dissuade the company from investing in new herbicide technologies altogether, the Journal reported.

data.title: Stem to cut 27% of global workforce in cost-cutting effort
data.description:
Stem (NYSE:STEM) +4.5% in early trading Monday after disclosing plans to reduce its global full-time workforce by 27%, saying it is part of broader efforts to prioritize investments in software, reduce operating costs, and increase efficiency.

Stem (STREM) said it faces a large debt burden, limited operating cash, and is not operationally profitable, leading to the decision to reduce the organization's workforce.

The company estimates $24M in related savings during partial year 2025 savings and $30M in savings during FY 2026, excluding an expected one-time GAAP pre-tax charge in Q2 of $6M-$6.5M, mostly related to severance payments and post-employment benefits.

Stem (NYSE:STEM) recently reported a FY 2024 GAAP loss of $5.29/share on $144M in revenues, and guided for FY 2025 revenues of $125M-$175M.

data.title: AtkinsRéalis acquires 70% stake in David Evans Enterprises
data.description:
• AtkinsRéalis Group (ATRL) has acquired a 70% stake in David Evans Enterprises, parent company of David Evans and Associates.
  • ATRL:CA is +1.32646% to $68.75.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: APA promotes Ben Rodgers to the post of CFO
data.description:
APA Corporation (NASDAQ:APA) announced on Monday the promotion of Ben Rodgers to the post of executive vice president and CFO, effective May 12.

Rodgers previously served as senior vice president, finance and treasurer.

data.title: MarketAxess reports record trading week in U.S. government bonds
data.description:
• MarketAxess Holdings (NASDAQ:MKTX) has announced that its Rates business reported an all-time single-day record of $102.3B U.S. Government Bonds notional traded on April 9.
  • “We’re thrilled to see this growth in our client business. We’re seeing clients increasingly seek out firm sources of liquidity like Live Markets to weather periods of market volatility like we experienced last week,” said Chris Good, Head of Rates at MarketAxess.
  • Following the launch of an expanded algo suite for Rates, MarketAxess also plans to introduce enhanced Request for Quote for U.S. Government Bonds, allowing dealers expanded reach through the MarketAxess client network.

data.title: Dollar edges higher after last week’s slump-Currency Recap
data.description:
The U.S. Dollar edged higher on Monday, walking back on some of its losses from last week, as markets scrambled to assess the impact of Trump’s uncertain tariff moves on the world’s reserve currency. 

The greenback was last up 0.14%, at $100.03, as Trump announced late on Friday that smartphones, computers, and other electronics would be temporarily exempted from tariffs. 

However, Trump said that the exemption was only temporary and that he would unveil tariffs on imported semiconductors soon. 

Dollar weekly moves and key drivers: 

The U.S. Dollar Index (DXY), which measures the value of the dollar against a basket of six foreign currencies, fell 3.26% over the last week. 

The greenback saw a sharp decline last week as investors fled U.S. assets due to tariff uncertainties. Although Trump announced a 90-day pause on tariffs for most countries, China still faces a significantly higher tariff rate, contributing to market volatility. 

China retaliated last week with a plan to raise tariffs on U.S. imports to 125%, from the previous 84%. This sent the U.S. dollar below $100 on Friday, reaching its lowest level since mid-2023. 

Adding pressure to the U.S. dollar, US Labor Department data showed an unexpected decline in consumer prices for March, and U.S. Treasury yields also felt the burn.

The benchmark U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) hit a two-month high on Friday, amid a bruising bond selloff in longer-term maturities. The US10Y surged as much as 19 basis points to hit a session peak at 4.58%, its highest level since February 12. 

Meanwhile, the shorter-end, 2-year yield (US2Y) also rose 19 points over the week to 3.94%. 

Currency Highlights: Major currency movements (April 7 to April 13) 

Euro (EUR:USD)

+3.72%

Pound Sterling (GBP:USD)

+2.58%

Japanese Yen (JPY:USD)

+3.10%

Chinese Yuan (CNY:USD)

+0.40%

Swiss Franc (CHF:USD)

+4.93%

Australian Dollar (AUD:USD)

+4.31%

Canadian Dollar (CAD:USD)

+2.28%

The Euro (EUR:USD) was last down 0.02% against the U.S. Dollar, but hovered close to Friday’s three-year high. The euro gained more than 3% during the last week as investors looked for a safe haven as confidence eroded in the dollar. 

Euro traders will keep a close eye on the European Central Bank policymakers’ latest meeting this week on interest rate decision. 

The British Pound (GBP:USD) was last up 0.33% against the greenback, extending its gains from last week, on the back of a weaker dollar. The pound will move further this week as the latest UK economic data comes in, such as unemployment and inflation numbers. 

The Japanese yen (JPY:USD) was up 0.39% against the dollar on Monday, as investors kept looking at the currency as a potential safe haven. 

Market reactions and analysis:

Deutsche Bank said last week that it sees the end of an era for the U.S. dollar. “We are witnessing a simultaneous collapse in the price of all U.S. assets including equities, the dollar versus alternative reserve FX and the bond market,” said George Saravelos, co-head of Foreign Exchange Research at Deutsche Bank. 

“In a typical crisis environment, the market would be hoarding dollar liquidity to secure funding for its underlying US asset base,” he said. However, the market seems to “have lost faith” in U.S. assets, and instead of buying them, they are being sold, he added.

Meanwhile, according to BofA, investors should short the S&P 500 until the Federal Reserve “cuts hard” to break the liquidation cycle, and until the U.S. and China pause their trade war and reverse global recession momentum.

BofA sees U.S. yields going higher and recommends investors to long the 2-year U.S. Treasuries (US2Y) and to short the S&P 500 (SP500) until the 4,800 level. 

Economic Indicators to watch: 

Investors will look out for retail sales data for March, which is due to come out this week. Other important economic data due this week, include housing market data, housing starts, and homebuilder confidence survey. 

Markets will also await earnings from several large financial firms this week, such as Goldman Sachs, Bank of America, and Citigroup, including results from Netflix, TSMC, United Airlines, and UnitedHealth Group. 

Dollar-Based ETFs: (UUP), (USDU), and (UDN).
